properties.mechanism: The count of enabled audience entities forms the targeting infrastructure that populates and feeds the audience signals configured at the asset group level to guide Google's AI.
Maintaining more enabled audiences improves targeting precision, which raises conversion rate and ROAS in campaign performance.
The subject's ROAS/CPA/CTR/ConvRate formulas are computed directly from Spend, Conversion Value (Revenue), Clicks, and Conversions, which are exactly the raw aggregates this metric supplies.
Higher Quality Scores improve ad rank and lower CPC, which raises CTR and lowers CPA, driving the subject's campaign performance.
Stronger responsive search ad strength wins more and better placements, lifting CTR and conversion rate in the subject table.
The daily spend/ROAS trend is the same campaign performance data sliced by day, so its values co-move with the aggregated subject metrics.
Device-level spend and ROAS are segment breakdowns of the same underlying campaign data, so they co-move with the overall campaign performance.
Reallocating budget share across campaign types shifts the blended ROAS/CPA/CTR because each campaign type converts at different efficiencies.
Campaign types map closely onto ad networks (Search, Display, YouTube/Video), so spend share by campaign type co-moves with spend distribution across networks.
Both metrics compute device-level spend and ROAS from the same COST_MICROS/CONVERSIONS_VALUE fields, so they co-move as the underlying per-device spend and conversion value shift.
Both slice performance by device, so device-level engagement (view rate by device) and device spend/ROAS tend to co-move as audience device mix changes.
Device spend and network spend are alternative partitions of the same total COST_MICROS, so both move together as overall account spend changes.
Higher quality scores lower CPC and raise impression eligibility, reducing device spend per conversion and thereby improving device ROAS.
Total daily account spend and ROAS are the sum of device-level spend and conversion value across all devices, so device performance is a component of the daily trend.
Total daily spend equals the sum of spend across ad network types, making network spend a direct component of the daily spend total.
Total daily spend is the sum of spend across campaign types, and the type spend-share metric is derived directly from that same total spend.
The daily account spend and ROAS aggregate the per-campaign spend and conversion value reported in the campaign performance table.
Higher quality scores lower CPC and improve ad rank, reducing daily spend per click and lifting the daily ROAS trend.
The choice of keywords and their match types drives expected CTR and ad/landing relevance, which Google uses to compute the Quality Scores that populate this distribution.
Match type breadth in the keyword portfolio determines which user queries are eligible to trigger ads and therefore which search terms surface in this report.
Both are controllable account build-out counts (enabled keywords vs. enabled audiences), so accounts that expand one structural asset tend to expand the other.
Allocating more spend to a given ad network buys more delivery on it, increasing that network's video views and thus its share of total views.
Both are Google ML assessments of ad/creative relevance, so accounts with stronger RSA Ad Strength tend to show better Quality Score distributions.
Higher Quality Scores lower CPC and raise expected CTR, improving the CPA and CTR observed across the top search terms.
Higher RSA ad strength from better asset diversity improves the ad-relevance component that feeds into Quality Score, shifting the score distribution upward.
Stronger responsive search ads earn more clicks per impression on matched queries, raising the search terms' CTR.
Search-term-level CTR and CPA aggregate up into the same campaign-level CTR and CPA, so the two move together.
Better audience signals sharpen Google's AI targeting, lifting conversions and conversion value captured in PMax performance metrics.
Stronger audience signals guide the AI toward higher-intent users, improving PMax campaign ROAS and lowering CPA.
As audience signals train the AI over time, PMax ROAS and spend efficiency in the performance trend improve.
Campaign Spend Trend is computed as SUM(SPEND) grouped by day and campaign, drawing directly on the same per-row Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS) value that Performance Metrics defines.
Both are PMax daily-spend time series on the same account, so total daily spend and top-campaign daily spend move together over the date range.
The account-level daily spend trend reliably co-moves with the per-campaign daily spend of the top campaigns that make up most of that spend.
Raising or cutting a campaign's daily spend causally changes that campaign's conversion value and CPA reported in the campaign performance table.
Campaign ROAS and CPA are computed directly from Spend, Conversion Value (Revenue), and Conversions, which this metric provides as raw summed components.
Campaign Spend is the denominator of Campaign ROAS and numerator of Campaign CPA, and this metric reports that per-campaign spend.
Both compute the same Conversion Value / Spend ROAS for PMax, so the campaign table and the 90-day trend co-move at the same grain.
Quarterly ROAS is the same Conversion Value / Spend ratio aggregated to quarters, so it tracks the campaign-level ROAS over time.
Device-level spend and ROAS are a breakdown of the same campaign spend and conversion value, so device totals co-move with the campaign aggregate.
Both compute device-level Spend and ROAS from the same COST_MICROS and CONVERSIONS_VALUE on the same Google Ads account, so they move together across device segments.
Device-level spend and conversion value are a partition of the overall PMax performance metrics' Spend and Revenue, so the device breakdown sums to and co-moves with the aggregate.
Device Spend and Device ROAS are derived from the same SUM(COST_MICROS) and SUM(CONVERSIONS_VALUE) raw fields that define the subject's Spend and Revenue.
Daily Spend and ROAS in the 90-day trend are computed from the subject's Spend and Conversion Value components aggregated by day.
Quarterly ROAS is the subject's Conversion Value divided by Spend, aggregated to the quarter.
Both compute the same PMax ROAS = Conversion Value / Spend over different time windows, so the 90-day trend and quarterly ROAS move together off the same underlying spend and conversion-value data.
Both share the identical Daily Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS)/1,000,000 and Daily ROAS formulas over the same account, so the daily series co-move closely.
Account-level ROAS is the spend-weighted aggregation of per-campaign ROAS, so campaign-table ROAS and the 90-day trend co-move.
Higher quality scores lower CPC and improve ad rank, reducing cost per conversion and raising ROAS through a real-world auction mechanism rather than a formula identity.
The 90-day performance trend computes the identical ROAS = Conversion Value / Spend on the same PMax account at a daily grain, so it reliably co-moves with the quarterly ROAS aggregation.
Quarterly account ROAS is the spend-weighted aggregation of per-campaign conversion value and spend, so shifts in individual campaign ROAS drive the account-level quarterly ROAS.
Campaign spend feeds the total Quarter Spend denominator, so increasing spend without a proportional rise in conversion value lowers Quarterly ROAS.
The Video Performance Trend computes the exact same View Rate, CPV, and ROAS formulas over time, so it co-moves with the subject's aggregate campaign performance.
Device-level View Rate and view share are segmentations of the same views and impressions that aggregate into the subject's overall View Rate, so they reliably co-move.
Network view share partitions the same total video views that feed the subject's View Rate and CPV, so network-level views co-move with the aggregate.
The heatmap reports average View Rate by hour and day, a temporal segmentation of the subject's View Rate that co-moves with the overall figure.
Higher video quartile-completion reflects stronger viewer engagement, which increases the number of counted video views relative to impressions and thus raises the subject's View Rate.
Both compute YouTube View Rate as Video Views over Impressions, so the device-level view rate and the time-trend view rate track the same underlying audience engagement and move together.
Campaign-level View Rate and device-level View Rate are the same engagement metric sliced along different dimensions, so they reliably co-move as overall video engagement shifts.

Device Share (Device Views / Total Views) and Network Share (Network Video Views / Total Video Views) are parallel share-of-views breakdowns drawn from the same total view pool, so they co-move as the view mix changes.
The hour/day heatmap averages the same View Rate that the device breakdown measures, so both slices reflect and co-move with the campaign's overall view-rate level.
Both report YouTube View Rate (Views/Impressions) over the same campaigns, so the hour/day heatmap cells co-move with the campaign-level view rate.
The performance trend tracks View Rate over time, the same underlying metric the heatmap segments by hour and day, so the two co-move.
View Rate by Device and View Rate by Hour/Day are the same View Rate metric sliced along different dimensions, so they statistically co-move.
The subject's denominator (Total Video Views) is the aggregate video-view count tracked by the video performance trend metric.
Both are share-of-total-views breakdowns (by network vs. by device) that co-move with overall YouTube view volume and audience mix.
The trend's View Rate, CPV, and ROAS are the same three metrics computed per campaign, so the overall video performance trend aggregates the campaign-level View Rate/CPV/ROAS over time.
Overall View Rate (Video Views / Impressions) is the impression-weighted sum of the per-device View Rates this metric reports, so device-level view rates are components of the aggregate trend.
This metric exposes the raw Spend and Revenue (Conversion Value) inputs that the subject's CPV and ROAS formulas are computed from.
Video completion/drop-off rates and View Rate are both viewer-engagement signals driven by creative quality, so they reliably co-move without one being a formula input of the other.
The heatmap is the same View Rate sliced by hour-and-day, so its cell values move together with the subject's aggregate View Rate trend.
Network-level video view share shifts alongside the overall video performance trend because both reflect the same underlying video-view volume across YouTube networks.
View Rate (Views/Impressions) and quartile completion both measure YouTube video engagement on the same impressions, so they reliably co-move.
The View Rate trend tracks the same viewer-engagement signal as quartile completion over time, so the two series move together.
Per-device View Rate reflects the same engagement that drives quartile completion, so devices with higher view rates tend to show higher completion.
View Rate by hour and day captures the same time-of-day engagement pattern that shapes quartile completion rates.
